nodes.name: 虎皮鸚鵡
nodes.summary: 虎皮鸚鵡（Budgerigar，学名 Melopsittacus undulatus），在图谱中标记为无危（LC）物种，已关联4个分布地点节点，常见栖息地包括灌丛、稀树草原。

nodes.name: 澳大利亚
nodes.summary: 澳大利亚是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联221种鸟类，包括南方鶴鴕、鵲鵝、细斑树鸭等221种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 约翰·古尔德
nodes.summary: 约翰·古尔德是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联71种鸟类，包括绿棉凫、凍原豆雁、黄腹角雉等71种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 乔治·肖
nodes.summary: 乔治·肖是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联14种鸟类，包括麝鴨、黄腹金鹃、髻鸠等14种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 熱帶雨林氣候
nodes.summary: 熱帶雨林氣候是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联1种鸟类，包括虎皮鸚鵡，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 灌丛
nodes.summary: 低矮木本植物占优势的陆地生境。

nodes.name: 稀树草原
nodes.summary: 东非常见的开放草地与湿地边缘复合景观。

nodes.name: LC
nodes.summary: LC表示 IUCN 无危等级，当前关联8208种鸟类，如鸵鸟、鸸鹋、南方鶴鴕等8208种鸟类。

links.evidence: |title=A budgerigar Melopsittacus undulatus from the Pliocene of Riversleigh, North-western Queensland |journal=Emu|date=1998 |volume=98 |issue=1 |pages=32–35 |doi=10.1071/MU98004|bibcode=1998EmuAO..98...32B }}}} | image = Budgerigar-male-strzelecki-qld.jpg | image_caption = Blue cere indicates male | image2 = Budgerigar-strzelecki-qld.jpg | image2_caption = Flaking brown cere indicates female in breeding condition | status = LC | status_system = IUCN3.1 | status_ref = | display_parents = 3 | genus = Melopsittacus | parent_authority = Gould, 1840 | species = undulatus | authority = (Shaw, 1805) | range_map = Budgerigar_range_map.png | range_map_caption = The range of the Budgerigar Native range: green Introduced range: light green }} The budgerigar ( ; Melopsittacus undulatus), also known as the common parakeet, shell parakeet or budgie ( ), is a small, long-tailed, seed-eating parrot native to Australia

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: 200px|thumb|right|Distribution map of budgerigar in Australia Distribution area ---- {| class="collapsible collapsed" !Climate region key |- | |} Budgerigars are nomadic and flocks move on from sites as environmental conditions change. Budgerigars are found in open habitats, primarily in scrublands, open woodlands, and grasslands of Australia. The birds are normally found in small flocks, but can form very large flocks under favourable conditions. The nomadic movement of the flocks is tied to the availability of food and water. Budgerigars have two distinct flight speeds which they are capable of switching between depending on the circumstance. Budgerigars sometimes swarm together in groups containing thousands of individuals. Drought can drive flocks into more wooded habitat or coastal areas
